fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CHRISTIE J:

This is an application for amendment of final parenting orders pursuant to the slip rule. The applicant is the mother of X born in 2023. The respondent is X’s father.
When the matter was before the Court for final hearing, X’s interests were represented by an Independent Children’s Lawyer. The making of final orders had the effect of discharging that order for appointment.
Rule 10.13 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) provides for variation of final orders. The provisions upon which the applicant relies upon are Rule 10.13(1)(e), 10.13(1)(g) and 10.13(1)(h). Those provisions relate to variation of orders where they do not reflect the court’s intention, where there has been a clerical error, or where there has been an accidental slip or omission.
The applicant seeks that the Court use Rule 10.13(1) to amend three of the orders made on 12 December 2025 after a final hearing.
Those orders are:
(a) Order 6;

(b) Order 7; and

(c) Order 9.

Those orders are set out below:
6. Orders 4-5 are suspended, and [X] spend time with each parent during the NSW school holiday periods, as agreed between the parents in writing and failing agreement:
(a) In the Term 1, 2 and 3 NSW School Holiday Periods: -
(i) In even numbered years, from 3.00pm or after school on the last day of school term until 3.00pm on the following Friday, being a period of not less than one week, with the father; and from 3.00pm on the Friday falling in the middle of the holiday period until 3.00pm on the Friday immediately prior to the commencement of the new school term, being a period of one week, with the mother.

(ii) In odd numbered years, from 3.00pm or after school on the last day of school term until 3.00pm on the following Friday, being a period of not less than one week, with the mother; and from 3.00pm on the Friday falling in the middle of the holiday period until 3.00pm on the Friday immediately prior to the commencement of the new school term, being a period of one week, with the father.

(b) In the Term 4 NSW School Holiday Periods, in even numbered years:
(i) From 3.00pm on the last day of the school term until 3.00pm on the midpoint day of the school holiday period with the mother;

(ii) From 3.00pm on the midpoint day of the school holiday period to 9.00am or before school on the first day of school, with the father.

(c) In the Term 4 NSW School Holiday Periods, in odd numbered years:
(i) From 3.00pm or after school on the last day of the school term until 3.00pm on the midpoint day of the school holiday period with the father;

(ii) From 3.00pm on the midpoint day of the school holiday period until 9.00 am or before school on the first day of school with the mother.

7. The above orders are suspended so that [X] spend time with both parents on occasions of special significance, as agreed between the parties in writing but failing agreement as follows:
(a) On Christmas Day and Boxing Day:
(i) With the mother in odd numbered years from 12.00 noon on 23 December to 12.00 noon on 26 December; and

(ii) With the father in even numbered years from 12.00 noon on 23 December to 12.00 noon 26 December.

(b) In the New Year Period:
(i) With the father in odd numbered years from 12.00 noon on 30 December to 12.00 noon on 1 January; and

(ii) With the mother in even numbered years from 12.00 noon on 30 December to 12.00 noon on 1 January.

(c) During the Easter period:
(i) In odd numbered years with the father from 3.00 pm or the conclusion of school the day prior to Good Friday until 3.00 pm on Easter Saturday if not otherwise with the father and thereafter with the mother from 3.00pm Easter Saturday until commencement of school the following Tuesday if not otherwise with the mother; and

(ii) In even numbered years with the mother from 3.00pm, or the conclusion of school, the day prior to Good Friday until 3.00pm Easter Saturday if not otherwise with the mother and thereafter with the father from 3.00pm Easter Saturday until commencement of school the following Tuesday if not otherwise with the father.

(d) On the Father's Day weekend, from 3.00 pm on the day prior to Father's Day to 9.00 am on the day following Father's Day, if not ordinarily with the father.

(e) On the Mother's Day weekend, from 3.00 pm on the day prior to Mother's Day to 9.00 am on the day following Mother's Day, if not ordinarily with the mother.

(f) If not ordinarily with a parent and [X’s] birthday falls on a school day, then [X] will spend time with that parent from 3.00 pm or the conclusion of school until 5.00 pm and in the event that it is a non-school day, then [X] will spend time with that parent from 9.00 am to 1.00 pm.

...

9. The mother shall facilitate [X] speaking to the father during periods [X] is in his care:
(a) At any time if she requests;

(b) On at least one occasion in periods where she is in the father’s care for three nights or more.

Order 9

It is simplest to begin with Order 9 which must be wrong on its face. The respondent agrees and I propose to make the amendment sought.

Order 6

Order 6 is about holiday time between X and the father. The mother says that consistent with the intention expressed in the reasons for judgment, the reference to order 4 is erroneous. The father agrees but for a different reason.
The mother draws attention to the following paragraph [115] of the reasons for judgment:
In the first instance, the time should be modest and in keeping with the existing interim arrangement. Given [X’s] age, the time should be regular and frequent to allow [X] the opportunity to develop her relationship with the father further. If they are seeing one another regularly then telephone/video calls can be fewer.
It is also necessary to have regard to paragraph 116:
The father’s application that [X] spend equal time with each parent is aspirational. It reflects what he would like to happen but given the complexities of the situation, it is not tenable nor could I, on the present evidence, predict whether it will be in her best interests at the time it is introduced.
The mother says that consistent with the content of paragraph 115, Order 6 could not have been intended to introduce extensive overnight time with the father (and away from the mother) at this stage. As earlier observed, the father understood that as being the intent and purpose of the orders. I propose to amend the orders to delete the reference to Order 4 but otherwise leave the reference to Order 5. This is accurate and consistent with the reasons. The order should be amended as sought.

Order 7

Order 7 is about special occasions. The reasons for judgment say at [118]:
The father’s proposal permitted [X] to celebrate important events with both parents such as Christmas and birthdays. The mother sought likewise in her alternative proposal and I have made orders accordingly. The parties’ proposals were not significantly different and I heard no particular submissions which would persuade me of relevant differences.
The applicant acknowledges that the slip rule only applies to those situations where the resulting orders unambiguously do not implement the intention of the trial judge. Here the situation as it concerns holidays and special occasions is not unambiguous.
The orders for periodic overnight time do not commence until 14 February 2026, which make Order 7(a)(ii) and Order 7(b)(i) inconsistent with the pattern established by Order 4.
However, Orders 7(c), 7(d) and 7(f) do not appear to fall foul of the conclusions expressed in the reasons for judgment.
The applicant contends that because Order 7 refers to “school” then it cannot be intended to operate since X is not yet at school. Ironically, given the concession made by the respondent about the other paragraph, I disagree - since at least here in Order 7, school is used in the alternative so that Order 7 applies both before X starts school and after she starts school.
Given the timing of when the special occasions are to occur and given the lack of extensive reasons for judgment by me on this issue, I am not satisfied that Order 7 could properly attract the slip rule. It falls into that category which is prohibited by the Rules. I accept it would require an exercise of discretion or reconsideration of at least some of the substance of the original decision consistent with the principles expressed by the Honourable Justice Schonell in Bhagat & Sandhu (No 5) [2025] FedCFamC1F 298.
Accordingly, the final orders will be amended as follows:
(a) Order 6 is amended to omit reference to Order 4;

(b) Order 9 is amended to read “the father shall facilitate X speaking to the mother during periods X is in his care.”
